topic: social media marketing for the shop

turns.content: alright i need help with something im not good at. social media for my shop
No problem. What kind of business is it, and what are you working with right now — do you have any pages set up already, or are you starting from zero?
auto repair shop. i got a facebook page but i havent posted on it in like 6 months. dont really know what to put on there

That's more common than you'd think. The good news is you don't need to be a marketing expert to make it work. For an auto shop, the best content is simple and real — before and after photos of jobs, quick tips about car maintenance, and the occasional post introducing your team. People want to trust their mechanic, and seeing the work builds that trust. How often do you think you could realistically post?
maybe twice a week if its not too complicated. i dont have time to be making fancy graphics or whatever

Twice a week is perfect. And forget fancy graphics. Just pull out your phone and snap a photo of a job you just finished, write two sentences about what you did, and post it. Something like a photo of new tires on a truck with "Got Mrs. Johnson road-ready for her trip. New set of all-terrains and an alignment." That kind of stuff is way more effective than polished ads because it feels real.
that actually sounds doable. what about google reviews. everybody says i need more of those

They're right. Google reviews are probably the single most important thing for a local auto shop. When someone searches for tire shops or mechanics near them, Google ranks you higher if you have more reviews with good ratings. The easiest way to get them is to ask customers right when they pick up their car. You can even print a little card with a QR code that takes them straight to your review page. Most people are happy to leave one if you just ask.
qr code. how do i even make one of those

It's easier than you'd think. First, find your shop on Google Maps and copy the link. Then go to a free site like qr-code-generator.com, paste the link in, and it makes the code for you. Download it, take it to any print shop or even print it yourself, and stick it on a card or a small sign by your register. Takes about ten minutes total.
ok i can handle that. what about paying for ads on facebook. is that worth it or a waste of money

For a local shop, a small Facebook ad budget can actually work well. Even 5 to 10 dollars a day targeted to people within a 10 or 15 mile radius of your shop can get you in front of a lot of local eyeballs. Start small, run it for a week, and see if you get any calls from it. The key is targeting — you only want people nearby, not the whole city. But I'd get your page active with regular posts first before spending money on ads. An ad that leads to a dead page doesn't help.
makes sense. get the page looking alive first then spend money pushing it. i shoulda done this stuff years ago

Better late than never. A lot of independent shops are in the same boat — great at the work, not so much at the marketing. Start with two posts this week and ask your next three happy customers for a Google review. Small steps add up fast. You don't need to go viral, you just need to show up when someone nearby needs tires or a brake job.
alright man i got a plan now. appreciate it. gonna start today before i talk myself out of it
That's the right move. Take a photo of whatever's on the lift right now and post it. First one's always the hardest. After that it becomes routine. Good luck with it.
